cards.title: Why we are less likely to help someone when others are around
cards.body: In an emergency, we often look to others to decide how to react. If everyone else is doing nothing, we assume help isn't needed. This is the bystander effect. In a classic 1968 study, when a participant thought they were the sole witness to a medical emergency, 85% took action. But when they believed four others were present, that number dropped to just 31%.

cards.title: Why asking for a favor makes people like you more
cards.body: It sounds backward: to make someone like you, you should ask them for a favor, not do one for them. This is the Ben Franklin effect. When we help someone, our brain resolves the cognitive dissonance of helping a stranger by concluding, "I must like them, otherwise why would I do this?" Benjamin Franklin famously used this trick to win over a hostile political rival.

cards.title: How a useless third option tricks you into spending more
cards.body: Imagine choosing between a small popcorn for $3 and a large for $7. You might pick the small. But if a cinema adds a "medium" for $6.50, the large suddenly looks like an incredible bargain. This is the decoy effect. Marketers introduce an asymmetric "decoy" option that is priced to make the most expensive option seem like the best value, steering your choice.

cards.title: Why your brain forgets things it knows it can search online
cards.body: Our brains are highly efficient organizers. When we know information is easily accessible online, we tend to forget the details and instead remember where to find it. Known as the Google effect or digital amnesia, a pioneering 2011 study showed that participants who believed their typed facts would be saved on a computer made far less effort to commit those facts to memory.

cards.title: Why we judge whole experiences by their endings
cards.body: When evaluating a past event—like a vacation or a medical procedure—our brains don't average out the entire experience. Instead, we heavily judge it based on two points: the most intense moment (the peak) and the very end. In a famous experiment, patients undergoing a painful procedure rated the overall pain as lower if the doctor added an extra, slightly less painful minute at the very end.

cards.title: Why we work less hard when we are in a group
cards.body: In 1913, French agricultural engineer Max Ringelmann discovered that when people pulled on a rope together, they didn't pull as hard as they did individually. This is social loafing. When pulling alone, people gave 100% effort. But in a group of eight, individual effort plummeted to just 49%. Because our personal contribution is hidden in a crowd, we unconsciously slack off, assuming others will carry the weight.

cards.title: Why we always underestimate how long a task will take
cards.body: Even when we know similar projects have run late in the past, we consistently underestimate how long our current tasks will take. Psychologists call this the planning fallacy. First proposed by Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, it occurs because we focus on the best-case scenario and ignore unexpected delays, treating our current project as unique rather than part of a historical pattern of delays.

cards.title: Why you think ads brainwash others but not you
cards.body: We are quick to believe that media campaigns, political propaganda, and advertisements heavily influence other people. However, we consistently believe that we ourselves are largely immune to these same influences. This cognitive bias is called the third-person effect. Studies show this gap in perception can lead people to support censorship of media they believe will harm others while leaving themselves unaffected.

cards.title: How easily we split into hostile "us versus them" groups
cards.body: It takes shockingly little to trigger tribalism. In the 1970s, psychologist Henri Tajfel showed that when participants were divided into arbitrary groups based on trivial criteria—like whether they preferred a painting by Klee or Kandinsky—they immediately began favoring their "in-group." Even when they had never met, they consistently awarded more points to members of their own group and penalized the "out-group."

cards.title: Why we blame people's character instead of their situation
cards.body: When a driver cuts you off, you probably assume they are an aggressive jerk. But when you cut someone off, you blame the situation—like being late or a hidden blind spot. This is the fundamental attribution error. We systematically overemphasize internal character traits to explain other people's behavior, while ignoring the powerful situational forces that shape our own actions.

quizzes.question: According to the Ben Franklin effect, how can you make someone like you more?
options: Do a nice favor for them
Ask them to do a favor for you
Ignore them until they notice you
Give them a small, expensive gift
quizzes.explanation: Asking someone for a favor forces their brain to resolve the inconsistency of helping a stranger by assuming they must actually like you.

quizzes.question: How does the peak-end rule suggest we evaluate past experiences?
options: We average the entire experience's pleasure or pain
We only remember the first few minutes
We focus on the most intense moment and the end
We recall only the most boring parts
quizzes.explanation: Our brains shortcut long evaluations by focusing heavily on the peak intensity of the event and how the experience wrapped up.

quizzes.question: Why does the planning fallacy occur, according to psychologists?
options: We deliberately lie to make ourselves look good
We focus on the best-case scenario and ignore past delays
We suffer from physical exhaustion while planning
We assume that time runs faster in the future
quizzes.explanation: We treat our current task as unique and plan for the ideal path, ignoring our own historical data showing that similar tasks usually run late.

quizzes.question: What is the third-person effect in psychology?
options: The belief that media influences others more than ourselves
The tendency to talk about ourselves in the third person
The ability to see things from an elevated, bird's-eye view
The assumption that others always agree with our opinions
quizzes.explanation: We tend to believe that persuasive messages and media have a greater impact on other people than on our own choices.

quizzes.question: What did Henri Tajfel's minimal group paradigm demonstrate about human behavior?
options: People only cooperate if they are genetically related
We form hostile 'us vs. them' biases even over completely trivial differences
Groups must spend years together to develop deep loyalty
People are naturally cooperative with all strangers regardless of groups
quizzes.explanation: Tajfel showed that even meaningless, temporary groupings (like painting preference) trigger instant favoritism toward one's own group.

quizzes.question: Which of these is an example of the fundamental attribution error?
options: Assuming a late coworker is lazy rather than stuck in bad traffic
Thinking everyone agrees with your political views
Believing you are immune to advertising
Forgetting where you parked because you were distracted
quizzes.explanation: The fundamental attribution error makes us blame a person's character for their behavior while ignoring the external situation they are dealing with.

guesses.prompt: In a classic 1968 study, what percentage of participants sought help for an emergency when they believed four other witnesses were present?
guesses.explanation: When participants believed they were the sole witness, 85% helped. But when they thought four others were present, diffusion of responsibility dropped that to just 31%.

guesses.prompt: In Max Ringelmann's classic study on social loafing, what percentage of their maximum individual strength did people exert when pulling a rope in a group of eight?
guesses.explanation: As group size increases, individual effort drops. In groups of eight, people pulled at only 49% of their potential capability.
